children.text:
Unknown speaker 1: New Year's and play
Nina Simone in the bedroom while we
twirl like conductors batons and point
to where the notes should have been.

Unknown speaker 1: We sometimes cry in corners and curse
the air for its lack of affection.

Unknown speaker 1: Our womb pleads for us to take
notice while we crush the past
underneath our heels and condemn
ourselves to the fire next time.

Unknown speaker 1: We sometimes punctuate
the earth with our beauty.

Unknown speaker 1: And challenge the galaxy with our
smiles we sometimes transform.
Unknown speaker 1: Because evolution is in us all.
Unknown speaker 1: And to prove Satan a liar.
Unknown speaker 1: And

Unknown speaker 1: we sometimes take life by the
hand and peel her like an orange.
Unknown speaker 1: Slowly, slowly.

Unknown speaker 2: Wendell
Otley, followed by Yapet Brinson.
Wendell Otley: Good afternoon.

Wendell Otley: My name is Wendell Otley, and um,
I want to thank you first before I
start, since I won't when I'm finished.
Wendell Otley: Um, my poem is Damnation.

Wendell Otley: Come from behind the protection of the
white sheets you've worn, the piles of the
ashes of the crosses you've burned, the

Wendell Otley: stinking rotting corpses of the niggers
you've lynched, and face your damnation.

Wendell Otley: Pretend that my problems don't
exist, that I'm just bitching
for no reason in particular.

Wendell Otley: That I'm full of shit, and
you will face damnation.

Wendell Otley: Continue to show an abundance of
my negative side, without giving
equal weight to my positive.

Wendell Otley: To show me as a menace to society, but
find no faults in the society I menace.

Wendell Otley: To arrest me for committing a
blue collar crime, but acquit
yourself of white collar crimes.
Wendell Otley: And you will suffer damnation.

Wendell Otley: Ask, why am I not happy being a lower
case letter in your capitalistic system?

Wendell Otley: Why am I not satisfied with the rats and
roaches that come with low income housing?

Wendell Otley: Why am I not filled from head to toe
with hot dog and apple pie chanting
USA every four years patriotism?

Wendell Otley: And I will say, because I didn't
ask to come to this damn nation.
Unknown speaker 2: Yafet
Brinson, followed by DJ Renegade.
Yafet Brinson: Hey, what up?
Yafet Brinson: How's it going?
Yafet Brinson: My name is Yafet.

Yafet Brinson: The poem is called Roman
Catholic Hit and Run.

Yafet Brinson: Yesterday, a black cop, while crossing
the street, was hit and killed by
two black nuns on a mountain bike.

Yafet Brinson: The cop dude didn't have a chance
to pull his trigger, call for
backup, down that day old donut,
or polish his plastic badge.
Yafet Brinson: You see, the cop wasn't paying attention.

Yafet Brinson: He crossed in between, not at
the green, saw nothing at all
of oncoming traffic, didn't even
look before stepping off the curb.
Yafet Brinson: Besides, the nuns had right of way anyway.

Yafet Brinson: And no, no funeral was given,
no investigation was done.

Yafet Brinson: All that remained were the skid marks
of the Roman Catholic nigger nuns.
Unknown speaker 2: D.
Unknown speaker 2: J.
Unknown speaker 2: Renegade, followed by Ta-Nehisi Coates.

D. J. Renegade: Somebody go
wake up Shakespeare and tell
him DJ Renegade's in the house.

D. J. Renegade: Subterranean night colored magi,
three moods in the mode of Miles.
Subterranean means underground.

D. J. Renegade: Deep, profound, and Miles
was one deep brother.

D. J. Renegade: Miles deep like a shaft, descending to
the mother load, blowing undersongs.

D. J. Renegade: Miles on tenor trumpet, tenor eleven
levels deeper than the next cat.

D. J. Renegade: Painting all up under the canvas,
making it blue bleed out the other side.

D. J. Renegade: Blowing solo, only we
hear his bedrock blues.
D. J. Renegade: Underground cultural resistance.

D. J. Renegade: A virtual virtuoso battling musical
mafiosos burrowing under their skin.

D. J. Renegade: Miles, the son of a dentist doing
root work with a hoodoo horn hollering
bebop toast like he was Petey

D. J. Renegade: Wheatstraw, Satchmo's son in law, or
Stagger Lee stabbing mediocrity with
a sharpened fifth, or a signifying

D. J. Renegade: junkie jumping cold turkey out the
lion's mouth, or shine below the deck
of the Titanic, bluing up the boilers.

D. J. Renegade: You see, Miles could blue like bird and
freight like train, early like bird and
night like train, wing like bird and
rail like train, tunneling underground.

D. J. Renegade: A night color is blacker than
a thousand asphalts and Miles
was one deep black brother.
D. J. Renegade: Black and mysterious as smoke.

D. J. Renegade: Black as the sky, round
midnight, round midnight.

D. J. Renegade: Black as the pot that
stewed a bitch's brood.

D. J. Renegade: Black and turning like a tire,
treadmarking musical notation
on the asphalt of life.

D. J. Renegade: Miles was slick as black ice, cool as
black snow, sweet as black cherries,
hitting harder A black Jack Hammer, black
Jack Johnson, or a black Jack Daniels.

D. J. Renegade: See, Miles was a Jack of spades, but an
ace at his trade, blowing night colors.

D. J. Renegade: Like deep black, triple black,
shiny black, cinder black, ashy
black, quarter black, mega black,
multi black, all shades of Miles.

D. J. Renegade: Shifting harmonic gears
in his chromatic Ferrari.

D. J. Renegade: Blowing with his black
turn to the audience.

D. J. Renegade: Speaking coolly Magi are priests,
wailing wizards, soloing sorcerers.

D. J. Renegade: And Miles was one deep
black magic brother.
D. J. Renegade: Miles, conjuring in the key of we.
D. J. Renegade: Magi, Magi, that's the confused ones.
D. J. Renegade: Miles is singular, they say.
D. J. Renegade: But Miles, we know you plural, you y'all.

D. J. Renegade: You be bop miles, hard bop
miles, cool miles, fusion miles.

D. J. Renegade: Lord, Lord, Lord, you reverend
miles, tonally testifying
from the book of the blues.

D. J. Renegade: Blowing muted magic like
chapter and verse, making a
joyful noise unto the Lord.

D. J. Renegade: And anybody else hip
enough to dig the scene?

D. J. Renegade: Magi Miles with crazy
styles, even sported a tutu.
D. J. Renegade: Miles, 1.
D. J. Renegade: 6 kilometers of cool.
D. J. Renegade: Miles, 5, 280 dancing feet.

D. J. Renegade: Miles, too much distance for their
musical rulers, pouring decades
of music like vintage wine.

D. J. Renegade: Miles, wily, never smiley, too
quick to hit and took no shit.

D. J. Renegade: Nobody's fool, sleek, satin,
cool, anciently new, kind of blue.
D. J. Renegade: Our subterranean, night colored magic.
Unknown speaker 2: Ta-Nehisi
Coates, followed by Dorothy Marie Wright.

Ta-Nehisi Coates: Um,
I am Ta Nehisi Coates.
Ta-Nehisi Coates: That's alright, everybody do it.
Ta-Nehisi Coates: Um, and I'm from Baltimore.

Ta-Nehisi Coates: Um, but I came down here, I
guess, with the DCP, because
that's where I go to school.

Ta-Nehisi Coates: I'm gonna read a piece
entitled, Last Night.
Ta-Nehisi Coates: It goes like this.

Ta-Nehisi Coates: On this dark of bottomless night, She
returns, touching, quiet, making us
Silhouette swimmers in an ocean of sheets.

Ta-Nehisi Coates: What I have missed is my hand
gracing tender up the arc of her
back and her whispering music
before the tune escapes my lips.

Ta-Nehisi Coates: What I have wanted is her so nuclear
with wanting that she melts sun and
paints the sky plum purple with passion.

Ta-Nehisi Coates: And the stroke is so good that
I jump crazy at the sudden of
my alarm as it screams reality
across the mess of a lonely room.

Ta-Nehisi Coates: I peep the digital demon whose
red eyes wail six o'clock.
Ta-Nehisi Coates: And now I know why I hate mornings.

Ta-Nehisi Coates: They always leave me
holding pillows like lovers.

Unknown speaker 2: Dorothy Marie
Rice followed by Michael Collins.

Dorothy Marie Rice: As I read this
poem, well, before I read this poem,
I want to make a comment to Bert,
my friend, and say, I won't explain.
Dorothy Marie Rice: He understands.

Dorothy Marie Rice: And also give homage to Gwendolyn
Brooks, who said, it's okay to
have a poem that's not crafted.
Dorothy Marie Rice: This was written this morning.
Dorothy Marie Rice: Ambrosia.
Dorothy Marie Rice: Hush.
Dorothy Marie Rice: Somebody's calling your name.

Dorothy Marie Rice: The first buzz you hear, tiny
as a raindrop, is the Scout.

Dorothy Marie Rice: She travels miles on
sturdy wings, thin as mica.
Dorothy Marie Rice: Miles through ozone drenched currents.
Dorothy Marie Rice: Miles past housing projects.
Dorothy Marie Rice: Miles.

Dorothy Marie Rice: Miles past bald headed little
boys, pot bellied little
girls, and weary grandmothers.

Dorothy Marie Rice: Finally, a field, wild flowers,
clover, she tongues the purple
sweetness, baptizes herself, returns
to her kin, miles and miles away.
Dorothy Marie Rice: Children, go where I send thee.

Dorothy Marie Rice: We swarm, nestle ourselves
in the bosom of mountains.

Dorothy Marie Rice: Our hymns, a chorus, reverberating,
breaking sound barriers.

Unknown speaker 2: Michael
Collins, followed by Tony Blackman.

Michael L. L. Collins:
My name is Michael L.
Michael L. L. Collins: L.
Michael L. L. Collins: Collins.
Michael L. L. Collins: I'm from Indianapolis, Indiana.

Michael L. L. Collins: I'm a jazz poet, and I love reading
jazz poems, and I love the fact that
out there there are not only other

Michael L. L. Collins: poets, but poets out there with
books, and poets out there teaching.

Michael L. L. Collins: So it means also that I have,
there's an audience that listens,
it's not just waiting here to read.

Michael L. L. Collins: But anyway, I read one time, and I,
like I said, I love doing my jazz
poems, I read one time in Indianapolis,

Michael L. L. Collins: I read one of my jazz poems, and Mari
was out in the audience, and uh, You
know, I thought I did pretty good.

Michael L. L. Collins: She said what you did
wasn't of any substance.
Michael L. L. Collins: I said, damn, you're right.

Michael L. L. Collins: Because I had read back to it
and it wasn't of any substance.

Michael L. L. Collins: She said it didn't address
any issues in the community.
Michael L. L. Collins: I said, damn, you're right.

Michael L. L. Collins: I said, uh, got to do a
little bit more writing.

Michael L. L. Collins: And there were some important
issues in the community and
important issues where I was at.
Michael L. L. Collins: And I was going to church at the time.

Michael L. L. Collins: So I thought I'd write a little bit
about the church, and uh, I wanted to
say, there's one important question when
you go to church that you need to ask.
Michael L. L. Collins: That is, ask, who gave the money?
Michael L. L. Collins: Ask, where does the money go?
Michael L. L. Collins: Now, sisters, ask the preacher.
Michael L. L. Collins: To read with you from the book of Mark.

Michael L. L. Collins: Now ask if the Bible is opened
when he opens the finance books.

Michael L. L. Collins: Ask if the message in one book can
answer the message in the other.

Michael L. L. Collins: Ask him to turn to chapter
12, verse 38 through 44.
Michael L. L. Collins: Ask him to preach from that passage.
Michael L. L. Collins: Ask them to preach it's meaning.
Michael L. L. Collins: Now ask,
Michael L. L. Collins: why are there no women trustees?

Michael L. L. Collins: Ask, why no sisters sit
on the deacon board?
Michael L. L. Collins: Ask, why brothers ask sisters not to ask?
Michael L. L. Collins: Where does the money go?
Michael L. L. Collins: Then, counter him.

Michael L. L. Collins: ount to him the number
of sisters in the church.

Michael L. L. Collins: Count to him the sisters who serve
on the usher board, who sing in the
choir, and tell him not to forget
those who teach in the Sunday schools.
Michael L. L. Collins: Yeah.

Michael L. L. Collins: Count to him the small
number of men in the church.

Michael L. L. Collins: Count to him the number of
times you've heard that only
brothers can count the money.

Michael L. L. Collins: They have to count the money
early in the afternoon.

Michael L. L. Collins: Because baseball,
basketball, football, boxing.
Michael L. L. Collins: I said boxing.
Michael L. L. Collins: They create conflicts.
Michael L. L. Collins: And they just can't stay late.
Michael L. L. Collins: Except,
Michael L. L. Collins: oh yeah, except.
Michael L. L. Collins: Except if there's a special service.
Michael L. L. Collins: Like that time the champ was called.
Michael L. L. Collins: Yeah, it was that special Sunday.

Michael L. L. Collins: The champ, Iron Mike Tyson was
called to walk down the aisle.
Michael L. L. Collins: And he gave an exceptional donation.
Michael L. L. Collins: All the brothers were there.
Michael L. L. Collins: Preacher was there.
Michael L. L. Collins: You should have been there
Michael L. L. Collins: to ask.
Michael L. L. Collins: Ask him to open that book again.
Michael L. L. Collins: Yeah, ask him to read that message again.

Michael L. L. Collins: Ask him to read the part about
when the collection was taken.

Michael L. L. Collins: Ask him to read the part about
when that multi multi millionaire
gave that multi multi money.
Michael L. L. Collins: And that little itty bitty woman.
Michael L. L. Collins: Oh, she gave a little itty bitty bit.
Michael L. L. Collins: Read your Bible now.
Michael L. L. Collins: Ask who the Bible said gave the most.
Michael L. L. Collins: Now, then ask.

Michael L. L. Collins: Ask if the church's Sunday school
teacher says that she's been raped.
Michael L. L. Collins: Raped by that same sports hero,
Michael L. L. Collins: Iron Mike Tyson.

Michael L. L. Collins: That same Seventh Commandment
breaking sports hero.
Michael L. L. Collins: Ask, who will the church believe?
Michael L. L. Collins: Ask, if his book is still open.
Michael L. L. Collins: Finance book
Michael L. L. Collins: or Bible book.
Michael L. L. Collins: Ask, who gave the money?
Michael L. L. Collins: Ask, where does that money go?

Unknown speaker 2: Toni Blackman,
followed by Jonathan Gray.
Toni Blackman, followed by Jonathan Gray.
Toni Blackman: The black woman's struggle.

Toni Blackman: Her head swings low like
Sweet's chariot, but she ain't
coming forth to carry you home.

Toni Blackman: She and her sister,
carrying too much as it is.
Toni Blackman: Hello, my name is Toni Blackman.
Toni Blackman: I want to give a shout out to E.
Toni Blackman: Ethelbert Miller.

Toni Blackman: One of the reasons that I've
been writing for so long.
Toni Blackman: Irving G.
Toni Blackman: Russell.
Toni Blackman: He's a very helpful brother.

Toni Blackman: I write a lot of four poems that have
maybe seven words in them at the most.

Toni Blackman: So, um, I think today that's
what I feel like reading.

Toni Blackman: And this one is called
Accident at the Church.

Toni Blackman: The steeple toppled into the parking
lot, causing the parishioners to scream,
to actually care about one another.
Toni Blackman: Spell number three.

Toni Blackman: I tried to write a poem about your
eyes, but you kept staring at me.
Toni Blackman: Intercourse.

Toni Blackman: Last night I made love to
myself for the very first time.

Toni Blackman: There was absolutely
nothing painful about it.
Toni Blackman: Every Sunday.

Toni Blackman: Miss Lucy in that old tired red velvet hat
struts the avenue as if it were the night.

Toni Blackman: And my final piece is written for my
brothers who chant the pussy song.

Toni Blackman: Obsessed with vaginal chants
of regions of acts of nonsense.
Toni Blackman: Not moving us forward, stagnant we remain.

Toni Blackman: Clutching to those concepts with
which they have brainwashed us,
with which they have controlled us.
Toni Blackman: Sex can be hypnotic if you allow it to be.

Toni Blackman: Sex can be an escape
if you allow it to be.

Toni Blackman: The only true escape
from reality is death.

Toni Blackman: And we are allowing it to
happen one by one by one by one.
Toni Blackman: We are an endangered species.
Toni Blackman: Sex is not for our people, no.
Toni Blackman: We cannot allow it to happen.
Toni Blackman: For we, we must make love.
Audience: Applause

Unknown speaker 2: Next we have Jonathan
Gray, followed by Kwame Alexander.
Jonathan Gray: Jonathan
Jonathan Gray: from Howard University.
Jonathan Gray: Peace to all the brothers from 8 Rock.

Jonathan Gray: Um, if you think this poem applies
to you, then it probably does.
Jonathan Gray: What?
Jonathan Gray: You don't really want to battle.

Jonathan Gray: Are you buggin Comin at me with some
old muley mouth bullshit about why can't
the brothers and sisters get along.
Jonathan Gray: Fuck that, fake punk.

Jonathan Gray: You know you only kickin that shit so you
can have sex with a pseudo revolutionary,
nappy headed, falsetto intellectual who is
jockin you because you have a nose ring.
Jonathan Gray: A nose ring.
Jonathan Gray: And you surely ain't no bull like I am.
Jonathan Gray: Preachin about respect.
Jonathan Gray: You don't respect what I stand for.

Jonathan Gray: Always talking about represent,
hmm, what the fuck do you represent?

Jonathan Gray: Bourgeois black trust fund baby
trying to be real, phony punk, what?
Jonathan Gray: I know you ain't saying nothing.

Jonathan Gray: Doing this rhythm and poetry
to rebel against your parents.
Jonathan Gray: I don't have parents.
Jonathan Gray: My mother dropped me off when I was 12.
Jonathan Gray: I was old.
Jonathan Gray: I have suppositions.
Jonathan Gray: And you want to battle me?
Jonathan Gray: Bitch ass nigga, step!

Jonathan Gray: I ain't got nothin to live
for, and nothin to lose.
Jonathan Gray: So what, nigga?
Jonathan Gray: Step.
Jonathan Gray: Yeah, that's what I thought.

Unknown speaker 2: Kwame Alexander,
followed by, uh, Nehassaiu deGannes.
Kwame Alexander: What's happenin

Kwame Alexander: Much props to my daughter,
Nandi, the creator.
Kwame Alexander: Poet from D.
Kwame Alexander: C., 8 Rock.
Kwame Alexander: It's your mug.
Kwame Alexander: Stage Black.

Kwame Alexander: My favorite piece of a journal slash
book that I'm editing called The Flow.
Kwame Alexander: New black poetry in motion.
Kwame Alexander: Manpower.
Kwame Alexander: Appetizer.
Kwame Alexander: Strawberries.
Kwame Alexander: Orange slices.
Kwame Alexander: Pineapples.
Kwame Alexander: Grapes.
Kwame Alexander: Apples.
Kwame Alexander: So I ask her, What about the watermelon?

Kwame Alexander: She reads me like a page out of
the Afro listic Guide to Nutrition.
Kwame Alexander: You never mix citrus fruits with melons.

Kwame Alexander: Start with the scratch,
and there will be no itch.

Kwame Alexander: We must return to the
old ways, to the old way.
Kwame Alexander: Abandon that which is new and confusing.

Kwame Alexander: Just because it's modern,
don't make it real.
Kwame Alexander: It just become temporary, like my job.

Kwame Alexander: Paid 5 G's a week to smile at corporate
fearing would be Tarzans with jungled
bellies who make 5 G's a day to smile

Kwame Alexander: at corporate wannabes who waste no time
making well thought of jokes about OJ.
Kwame Alexander: Obey your thirst, drink Sprite.
Kwame Alexander: Obey your drink, thirst Sprite.
Kwame Alexander: Drink your Sprite, obey thirst.
Kwame Alexander: Thirst your drink, obey Sprite.
Kwame Alexander: Obey Sprite, obey Sprite.

Kwame Alexander: The blacker the smile,
the sweeter the pension.

Kwame Alexander: The slower the walk,
the faster the lynching.
Kwame Alexander: We remember Roots, the latest version.

Kwame Alexander: Written, directed, produced, conceived,
created, marketed, and starring.
Kwame Alexander: Introduction, I don't recall.

Kwame Alexander: Part one, the untouchable,
airborne nigger.

Kwame Alexander: Part two, the innocent, crotch
grabbing, more than black nigger.
Kwame Alexander: And the saga continues with part three.

Kwame Alexander: The most recent
unstoppable airport nigger.
Kwame Alexander: Caution, niggers.

Kwame Alexander: Mixing citrus fruits with melons is
hazardous to you and your health.
Kwame Alexander: Lately, everything has become sexual.

Kwame Alexander: The way you walk, the way we talk,
and even my job has become sexual.

Kwame Alexander: Want to stick my disc in the
drive and load my software.
Kwame Alexander: Error.
Kwame Alexander: Unable to read the disc.
Kwame Alexander: The program worked last week.

Kwame Alexander: So I checked my hardware and discovered
that the problem is my memory.

Kwame Alexander: I discovered that the
problem is my memory.
Kwame Alexander: Was it French or Italian?

Kwame Alexander: The tomatoes and olives should
be seasoned with feta cheese.

Kwame Alexander: Break the lettuce into half
inch pieces and then add the
croutons and the radishes.
Kwame Alexander: Mix everything in one bowl and chill.

Kwame Alexander: We walked the slow walk, the
walk of zombies and snow.

Kwame Alexander: We don't owe nobody, don't own
nobody, and don't know nobody
who listens to Garth Brooks.

Kwame Alexander: Khaled Mohamed maybe,
but not Garth Brooks.

Kwame Alexander: Leonard Jeffries maybe,
but not Garth Brooks.

Kwame Alexander: Maulana Karenga maybe,
but not Garth Brooks.

Kwame Alexander: Professor Egypt himself,
but not Garth Brooks.

Kwame Alexander: How you gonna practice an ancient
philosophy in 1994 and expect
it to work for you when it
didn't work for them in four?
Kwame Alexander: Silly rabbits, Afrocentrics are for kids.

Kwame Alexander: Grownups walk in the sun, avoid
snow at all costs, and attend narrow
nationalist pre Afro historic lectures
as much as they listen to Garth Brooks.
Kwame Alexander: The phone rings.
Kwame Alexander: It's 6.
Kwame Alexander: 30.
Kwame Alexander: It's not her.

Kwame Alexander: Fresh green peppers, diced onions,
marinated in tamari overnight.

Kwame Alexander: Mushrooms sautéed in olive
oil for three minutes.

Kwame Alexander: Fresh green peppers, diced onions,
marinated in tamari overnight.

Kwame Alexander: Mushrooms sautéed in olive
oil for three minutes.

Kwame Alexander: Fresh green peppers, diced onions,
marinated in tamari overnight, mushrooms
sautéed in olive oil for three minutes.

Kwame Alexander: I removed a package of Japanese stir
fried vegetables from their freezer,
where they had been sitting overnight.
Kwame Alexander: Enter mock duck.
Kwame Alexander: Not real duck, fake duck.
Kwame Alexander: Fake deal.
Kwame Alexander: Real late.
Kwame Alexander: She's supposed to be here.
Kwame Alexander: I'm supposed to be finished.

Kwame Alexander: Supposing it's 8 o'clock
and she still ain't here.
Kwame Alexander: Supposing the game is on in one hour.
Kwame Alexander: Supposing she comes at 8.
Kwame Alexander: 45 and expects to eat.
Kwame Alexander: Supposing she don't call.
Kwame Alexander: She don't.
Kwame Alexander: Supposing I sit down to eat by myself.

Kwame Alexander: Supposing I finish eating and
sit down to watch the game.

Kwame Alexander: Supposing I find that the game has
been preempted by another game.
Kwame Alexander: I thought he retired.
Kwame Alexander: Maybe him just tired.
Kwame Alexander: Replenish the body, black people.
Kwame Alexander: Feed the mind, African people.
Kwame Alexander: Reawaken the soul, Sun people.
Kwame Alexander: Chocula for the children.
Kwame Alexander: Food for the dissatisfied buppies.
Kwame Alexander: Chocula for the CBC.
Kwame Alexander: Food for the NAACP.
Kwame Alexander: BET.
Kwame Alexander: BDP.
Kwame Alexander: BBD.
Kwame Alexander: You and me.

Kwame Alexander: Everything that survives
on this planet needs food.
Kwame Alexander: Ask the questions, poets.
Kwame Alexander: Answer the questions, scientists.
Kwame Alexander: Probe the mind.
Kwame Alexander: Teach the truth.
Kwame Alexander: Grow the child.
Kwame Alexander: Till the soil.
Kwame Alexander: Plant the earth.
Kwame Alexander: Work the body.
Kwame Alexander: Study the mind.
Kwame Alexander: Watch the people.
Kwame Alexander: Love the people.
Kwame Alexander: Cross the desert of your black mind.
Kwame Alexander: Cross the desert of your mind.
Kwame Alexander: Sprinkle water on black brain cells.
Kwame Alexander: Sprinkle water on your brain cells.
Kwame Alexander: Reward your desert with spring water.

Kwame Alexander: Return to the way of the flow
of the water of our people.
Kwame Alexander: Beware.

Kwame Alexander: The wrong river will take you to
distant places like wacko Texas, and
South Carolina highways, and 7 by 9
foot cells, and separated marriages,

Kwame Alexander: and lonely nights, and tapped phones,
and repossessed cars, and ripped
condoms, and overdue flowers, and 9

Kwame Alexander: to 5s, and renovated plantations, and
late retirements, and multiple organ
failures, and late dates, and late dates.
Kwame Alexander: And late dates.

Kwame Alexander: Glancing on the kitchen table, I
see my paste up, wondering, when
will I get tired of this job?
Kwame Alexander: The three month itch is almost up.

Kwame Alexander: When will I retire, or better
yet, when will they tire of me?

Kwame Alexander: I mix the citrus fruits with the
melons, the nicks with the rockets,
and escape to my TV room where I eat

Kwame Alexander: three months of insecurity, pain,
laughter, and joy in three minutes.

Kwame Alexander: I stood up, contemplate whether
leaving the juice would make
me any less Afro neolistic.
Kwame Alexander: Suddenly, the phone rings at 10.
Kwame Alexander: 30.
Kwame Alexander: I answer.
Kwame Alexander: The voice asks, How are you?
Kwame Alexander: It's her.

Kwame Alexander: She who doesn't see the wine, and
the glasses, and the vegetables,
and the duck, and the juice,
and the juice, and my tears.

Kwame Alexander: Didn't I tell you I had
to get my hair done today?
Kwame Alexander: Hair today, gone tomorrow.
Kwame Alexander: She asks, What's on my mind?

Kwame Alexander: I hesitate, wondering whether I
should tell her that I mix the
citrus fruits with the melons.

Kwame Alexander: Instead, I choose to calm her
unnecessary guilt ridden simplicity
with the fruitful culmination of
my evening long introspection.

Erica Jackson: Good afternoon.
Erica Jackson: Um,

Erica Jackson: these are two parts from a five part

Tiles and it stems from an assault that

Erica Jackson: I suffered in 92 by a boyfriend that

Erica Jackson: And the first part is the third

Erica Jackson: It's called Stained Glass.

Erica Jackson: I fell into a friend last

Erica Jackson: His laughter and fraternally engraved body

Erica Jackson: My breath awakened the red hiding

Erica Jackson: Faced to cold glass, I clawed

Erica Jackson: and I coped.

Erica Jackson: And, uh, this is the last part.

Erica Jackson: Um, it, it, I can't explain it,

Erica Jackson: Um, I wrote it from the

Erica Jackson: Any other way I couldn't

Erica Jackson: His skin felt good against my branches.

Erica Jackson: A blue satin ribbon that danced
around my trunk and played hide
and seek through my leaves.
Erica Jackson: He gave me joy.

Erica Jackson: For years he played with me,
around me, under me, in me.
Erica Jackson: Until the day I saw his eyes.
Erica Jackson: Blue eyes.
Erica Jackson: Satin blue.
Erica Jackson: Poisonous satin.

Erica Jackson: Hypnotic blue shroud cracked revealing
satin tentacles that overpowered my
branches, leaves forming mock barriers.
Erica Jackson: Disgraced.
Erica Jackson: My fruit is violated.

Erica Jackson: Rooted here in his moment,
waning each time the wind sojoins
through my decaying trunk.

Erica Jackson: A leaf collapses, asking, Does
stolen fruit still taste as sweet?
Erica Jackson: Thank you.

Unknown speaker 2: Tyepha
Walden, followed by Nakia Green.

Tyepha Walden: Hello, my
name is Tyepha Walden.

Tyepha Walden: And today I'm going to read
a poem called My First Love.

Tyepha Walden: It's a personal letter I had
written to God this summer.

Tyepha Walden: It pains me to know all the
troubles I've been through.

Tyepha Walden: I've turned to alcohol, to sex,
and even to death, before I
turned to you, my first love.

Tyepha Walden: Sometimes I feel ashamed having
gone against your wishes,
time after time after time.

Tyepha Walden: All the things you've given me,
all the gifts you've given me,
excuse me, I had taken for granted.

Tyepha Walden: But still, you continue to
care for me, my first love.
Tyepha Walden: Once I had a terrible experience.
Tyepha Walden: I feared you did not love me anymore.

Tyepha Walden: For months and months I pondered
the thought of living without you.
Tyepha Walden: Oh, how terrible was that thought.
Tyepha Walden: For you are the very essence of my life.
Tyepha Walden: You are the reason I can wake up smiling.
Tyepha Walden: My ever so near, ever so dear, first love.

Tyepha Walden: You've always cared for me
when I didn't care for myself.

Tyepha Walden: You've always looked out for me
when I didn't look out for myself.

Tyepha Walden: And you've always loved me
when I didn't love myself.
Tyepha Walden: My first and only love.
Tyepha Walden: I'm sorry for all the pain I caused you.
Tyepha Walden: I have no excuse.

Tyepha Walden: But on this day, this very moment,
I have this promise for you.

Tyepha Walden: That I will never leave,
nor stop loving you.
Tyepha Walden: My thoughts shall be our thoughts.
Tyepha Walden: My words shall be our words.
Tyepha Walden: And my actions shall be our actions.
Tyepha Walden: For you were, and are, and always will be.

Tyepha Walden: My parent, my friend, my guidance,
my strength, and my redeemer.
Tyepha Walden: But above all, my first love.

Unknown speaker 2: Monifa A.
Unknown speaker 2: Love,

Unknown speaker 2: followed by, finally,
for, who is Tim Wells.
Monifa A. Love: Good afternoon, everyone.

Monifa A. Love: This poem is entitled Maximum Security,
and it's dedicated to Evans Hopkins, a
brilliant writer who is doing life in
a Brookville, Virginia penitentiary.
Monifa A. Love: This darkness doesn't scare me.

Monifa A. Love: No darkness can after Quang Tri Province,
that sticky, heavy night, when there
was no moon, when I had no eyes, when

Monifa A. Love: I had no hands, when everything was
pitch, and the smell of death was strong.

Monifa A. Love: No one died that night, but our fear
had us in body bags, on choppers, in
coffins, in the ground, back home.
Monifa A. Love: There was jasmine in the air.
Monifa A. Love: No one smelled it.

Monifa A. Love: I have stories I can tell
myself to mark the hours.

Monifa A. Love: I will know when it is
morning when it is light.

Monifa A. Love: Even if there are no sounds
outside this darkness, I will know.

Monifa A. Love: I will feel the surge of waking
restless bodies through the walls.
Monifa A. Love: I will feel them as they work.

Monifa A. Love: The weights work, the
tears work themselves.

Monifa A. Love: I will know, and I will feel
them struggling through the
night, calling out, calling.

Monifa A. Love: I have learned from sitting with
men in the dark, with the young
hopeful farmers in Bluefields, the

Monifa A. Love: old patient ones in Four Corners,
the nervous marketeers in Manila.
Monifa A. Love: I have learned how to rein myself in.
Monifa A. Love: I have learned how to watch.

Monifa A. Love: Circumspectly, I have learned how
to let what is going to happen come.
Monifa A. Love: This darkness doesn't scare me.
Monifa A. Love: No darkness can after Quang Tri Province.

Monifa A. Love: That cool, crystal night when the moon
sat plump and regal on a throne of clouds.

Monifa A. Love: When sparks crackled along
the surface of my skin.

Monifa A. Love: When everything was desire,
keen edged and elastic.
Monifa A. Love: We all died that night and woke reformed.
Monifa A. Love: There was jasmine in the air.

Monifa A. Love: We lifted our heads to catch
the fragrance, our eyes shut
to better find the scent.

Monifa A. Love: And we let it take us to our
memories, winsome, fragile
memories of love and coupling.

Monifa A. Love: I could see my small, thin, gap
toothed girl letting my unskilled
lips and tongue taste her smooth skin.
Monifa A. Love: Seeing her then.

Monifa A. Love: I reached for her, my
handheld invisible fruit.

Monifa A. Love: Yes, I have stories I can
tell myself to mark the hours.

Monifa A. Love: I will feel the falsetto
songs of the morning.

Monifa A. Love: I will feel men turning
from sleep and reaching.

Monifa A. Love: I will feel the repetition of
prayers, the flood of invectives,
the proclamations of survival.

Monifa A. Love: I will know when it is
morning and I will be fine.

Monifa A. Love: I will feel men touching
their tattooed places.

Monifa A. Love: And I will feel them rub the history
they have pushed into their skin.

Monifa A. Love: I have learned from sitting
with men in the dark.
Monifa A. Love: The frightened.
Monifa A. Love: Resigned.
Monifa A. Love: Betrayed.
Monifa A. Love: And ecstatic.
Monifa A. Love: I have learned how to set myself flying.

Monifa A. Love: I have learned how to
swim and to become water.
Monifa A. Love: I have learned how to billow.
Monifa A. Love: No darkness can scare me.
Monifa A. Love: Not after Quang Tri Province.
Monifa A. Love: Because I have learned to incandesce.
